If you want this deal to go through, you must put something worth the risk on the table.
Thank you for agreeing to meet with me, Mr. Bader. I will consider your proposal.
Time waits for no one. You must make your decision soon.
Their asking price is high.
We've discussed this situation. The Royal Mint is in Sarkaz hands. News of this must've already spread throughout the entire country via the Magna bankers.
Merchant smugglers are worried that the payment they earn will be worthless by this time next month. Naturally, they demand a method of payment that will keep its value in the long term.
You intend to accept his terms?
Mr. Bader was right. 'Time waits for no one.'
We have at most five more days to prepare. If we are to ambush the Defense Forces' command tower, we need to bring more weapons into the city.
But... what they demand is very dear to you.
Hah.
But this is the position we are in, Clovisia.
This is the position I am in.
How would you figure what it means to me?
I'm afraid...
No, I can say this for sure. Your speculations are all wrong.

Do you think of me simply as the magnificent Duke of Cumberland?
You likely weren't even born yet, but I...
I saw them hang Victoria's magnificent Lion King in the palace gardens with my own two eyes.
My name is Cumberland, and everyone knows Cumberland was His Majesty's most faithful friend.
Do you think the reason I stand here in front of you completely unharmed is because the bigwigs in Parliament had mercy on me?
Even now, I still regularly attend Lt. Colonel Lettou's cocktail parties. My words still carry some weight there, and they still need me to soothe the masses.
Of course, this is for our cause. It is also my own means.
I understand your sacrifice. Without your protection, the Self-Salvation Corps could not have made it to this day.
I'm not a girl anymore...
That thing in the attic means nothing.
It is a pile of scrap.
...Ailshie.
Make the arrangements and have it brought down from the attic.
You disagree with my decision?
I dare not speak, Miss.
I demand it of you.
Lady Allerdale, no matter what you say, it... it's still House Cumberland's steam armor!
The blood of your ancestors—that most esteemed Duke's blood flows within that armor. It is an honor that Victoria bestowed upon House Cumberland, and it is the symbol of your house, 'Ever on High.'
It can be destroyed in the flames of war, it can be given to another who is noble and unsullied. It can't... It can't be traded to a greedy merchant.
You don't think Mr. Bader is worthy?
An ancient set of armor! Treated as a lowly collectible! And then traded away at a bargain price for just—
Ailshie, you've been at my side all these years, ever since I was born.
We've kept the long fallen 'House Cumberland' alive.
My house was built on nothing but a bunch of empty words. 'Glory,' 'loyalty,' 'nobility,' or perhaps, 'benevolence?' But none of them hold any meaning today.
It's you and I upon whom this house now stands.
I understand, Miss.
Have we gotten a letter from them today...?
Not yet.
You know? The music of the past... as if it still rings loud in my head.
This manor once hosted a king, but where is he now?
My apologies, Your Highness—
...Call me Vina.
Welcome, Vina.
I've returned from patrolling with the fighters.
You're still willing to go on missions with the fighters of the Self-Salvation Corps. Splendid.
I want to do as much as I can.
It's more than enough for the men to simply know you are here.
I've heard from Clovisia...
Hah, the attic is absolutely cluttered with rubbish. I've got to pull myself together and clean up a bit.
Lots of dust there. I mustn't have Your Highness helping me with such things.
Allerdale.
I don't want you to sell the armor.
Is that an edict from Your Highness?
I have no right to issue one. You're the one responsible for the Self-Salvation Corps, not me.
You know better than I what's right for the Corps, and I understand that the armor can buy us an opportunity.
But... Please give me some time.
I will find a way.
Vina... You needn't worry yourself over this.
Is that an order from the Self-Salvation Corps' Auchterigg District leader?
Very well.
If nothing ever happened, perhaps...
Never mind, it's all in the past.
Lady Heidi!
What is it? Do we have any news from Golding?
Yes, she just sent us a message through the transmitter at the bookstore. It's been forwarded to your terminal.
Good... Very good.
She says she's safe. Everything in Auchterigg is proceeding according to plan.
Please send a reply. The Doctor's squad has successfully slipped past the checkpoint and infiltrated Highbury. Thanks to the mercenaries working with Rhodes Island...
Catherine, there's a problem with the conveyor belt at Loading Area No. 9!
Let's have a look.
There's a loose nut.
Patto!
I'm here.
You're on shift today, and we're running shorter and shorter on time. What are you doing?
If we don't speed things up, the Sarkaz will smear your brains across this steel here!
Sorry, Catherine.
Now hand me the spanner.
Okay...
Here.
Quick on your feet, all of a sudden...
You.
Long time no see, Granny.
I'm back.
Patto!
Catherine...
See your team leader after work for a write-up.
What?!
You let unauthorized personnel into the factory's most important landing zone. You're lucky you haven't been sacked.
Granny, this isn't Patto's fault. It wasn't him who let me in.
Good. Patto, bring Tommy and Day with you to get written up. I'd better not see a single one of you trying to wiggle out of this.
You, and the strange character from god-knows-where next to you...
Come.

lines.st:
Act 3
——————
Scene 7

lines.x: Sir, why is the palace so quiet?
Palace? What palace?
You are facing the outer walls of the palace.
Move aside, big heap of metal. You're blocking the sunlight.
I'm facing nothing but an empty house.
I am a Steam Knight. I purge all evil and injustice!
Young man, it's over.
No, it is not, nor shall it ever be!
So long as we still know what is right and what is wrong, so long as we never hesitate to make the right decision, it shall never be over!
I made my way here precisely for that reason. I invite all men to join me!
Even you have yet to relax your tightly clenched fist, am I right?
Amiya, our cover's blown!
How many hostiles?
Recon operators report four mercenary patrol teams headed in our direction.
We have to pass through the district to reach the Defense Forces' command tower.
And we've only just made it inside...
The Sarkaz forces are way too quick to react. We're being ambushed.
How is the backup route looking?
If the mercenaries have been lured to the front here...
No, it won't work!
There's another squad coming up fast! The Self-Salvation Corps just spotted them!
What did they say?
They had a run-in with this squad when they evacuated from Sudean! They say these Sarkaz fight... well they're...
Inhuman.
The Vampire's men...
Notify all units. The operation has been cancelled. Prepare to pull out immediately.
There's no time. The Vampire fighters are moving way too fast... The Self-Salvation Corps has engaged them!
Change of plans. We're aiding the Self-Salvation Corps!
Where's the Cautus?
What Cautus?
Don't play dumb with me. She's been with you lot the last couple days.
I've no idea what you're talking about, and I'm not talking. Not even if you kill me.
Another stubborn bunch.
Urghh—! What's this... Bugs?! Bloody...!
Hang in there, friend!
Back in formation. Don't let the bugs on the ground separate you.
Defender operators, cover our allies' retreat!
Rhodes Island and the Self-Salvation Corps came here together, and so we will leave together!
We have a signal, Amiya!
Good.
Casters! Hold the shield with me!
Everyone else, get to the corner!
The Sarkaz casters raise their staves together.
Monsters made of blood rush up from underground. The entire street opens its gaping maw beneath the soldiers.
Nngh—!
There's too many of them!
Black lines cut through the flesh of the monsters, but even more rise again from the fallen, gnawing away at the Arts forming a defensive barrier.
The fighters run for their lives, but the stench from the predators' jaws is never far behind.
Finally, they make it into another alley, and find themselves face-to-face with an aura that is potent, pure and fearsome.
Stop.
The bloody tide stops in its tracks.
It was as though they ran into an invisible wall when they rounded the corner. The maggot-shaped Arts creations smash themselves into pieces, leaving bloody trails on the ground.
The empty street is completely silent.
Casualties?
We're fine. We got out quick enough.
I hope we've delayed the Sarkaz, made things safer for Allerdale and Siege...
Thanks, everyone, especially our Self-Salvation Corps friends. Thank you for being willing to take this risk with us.
Commander Clovisia calls Rhodes Island our most important ally. We're always ready to do what we have to.
Plus, if this Rhodes Island operator here hadn't helped us, that Vampire would've done all of us in a long time ago.
No need to thank me.
I've seen too much meaningless killing in Londinium. I must stop my compatriots who have gone too far.
Sorry, Doctor...

opts: I thought I was just coming home with you.

lines.x: I had, er, a little fight with Granny when I skipped out of the factory last year.
A dozen big, bruising workers surround you.
Their eyes are filled with suspicion.
You glance at the drills and spanners in their hands, then at Feist, dressed like the workers, but here on his own.

opts: Seems like it was more than a little fight.
Are you sure they're not hostiles?

lines.x: I guess Granny is still sore at me...
I've got no reason to be sore at a dead man. I didn't even know my only grandson was still alive, 'til a minute ago.
I wanted to send a message, but it was too risky. The Sarkaz monitor all communications into Highbury.
I can't let the Sarkaz tie you to the Self-Salvation Corps.
So you joined that band of crackpots.
...The Londinium Citizens' Self-Salvation Corps.
We did a lot in Sudean, Granny. The Sarkaz control the media, and they call us an 'unnamed violent gang'...
You're the ones who blew up the wall.
Er... it was the Do...

opts: The Sarkaz damaged the walls while pursuing the Corps.
Your grandson fought bravely in the battle.

lines.x: Right... it was mostly the Sarkaz who did it.
Thanks, Doctor.
I didn't know you had such high regard for my rope work.
So you're here to blow up the armory?
Of course not!
What did you say to your Self-Salvation Corps friends? We're making weapons for the Sarkaz, to use against Victorians?
No, Granny. What I told them was...
My granny stands up to protect their livelihoods.
I know you think I'm a brat with little more than a couple of silly tricks.
But I didn't come back to drag the workers into this. And I'm not here to try to convince you, either.
I have my goals, of course, and it'd be brilliant if you were able to help me. But first—
I just wanted to see that you're safe.
Come here, and let me have a look at you.
Granny...
You've put on a few inches, brat.
Yeah, I grew a little bit.
I'll have the goods soon.
A suit of steam armor... how many men would kill to get one like it?
The nobles of Leithanien love to flaunt such storied antiques.
Oh, and do be sure to ask those Columbian firms as well. They might be interested, even if the tech is centuries old.
Getting it out will be no trouble at all. What business do you think I'm running in Londinium these days?
The Sarkaz are a gang of raving psychopaths, and they alone can't rule Londinium.
I have friends in the Defense Forces. They'll take care of it for me.
I've heard it has a few external blemishes, however. I do hope that won't hurt the price?
Why not cancel the deal, if you're so worried about the price?
Who's there?!
Don't worry, we just happened to be traveling the same way.
Get this intruder out of here!
Say... where are my bodyguards?
I've asked them to take a short nap. You can go and wake them once we've finished our chat.
All of them...? Against just one of you?!
One? Oh, my, no.
Look behind you.
You... this is Londinium! I've got friends in high places!
Oh? Where are they? Do tell, I bet I'd get on famously.
Eek! D-Don't do anything rash, now!
Play nice with us and nobody has to get beat.
Whatever Siege says, you do.
Otherwise, my claws won't be aimed at the wall next time.
A-Alright! I understand!
...Seriously?
You really got into it there.
I learned it from you lot.
Now then, let him go.
*whimper*...
You can open your eyes and put your hands down now, Mr. Bader. We're not going to hurt you... at least not yet.
What do you want?
My terms are clear. Cancel the deal for the Cumberland steam armor.
And you'll let me go if I agree?
Yes.
But I'll make sure you keep your side of the bargain.
Show Mr. Bader our map, Morgan.
With pleasure.
The marks on the map... those are my warehouses! How do you know about them?!
You don't pay much attention when you're out and about.
You shadowed me?
Correct. The Sarkaz cannot be bribed. If they learn that you've smuggled so much contraband into Londinium through your 'connections'...
How helpful do you think your friend will be? Will he be in any position to protect you?
You're considering tipping off the Sarkaz.
That may well get us all killed, but they won't have to know anything if you continue to work with us.
Do I have a choice?
I understand your circumstances, and I appreciate the opportunity to work with you. You will be compensated fairly.
Wait a second...
What is your relationship to Allerdale Cumberland? Are you her attendant? I can't imagine House Cumberland is in any position to hire one...
Watch your mouth! The Cumberland serves Vina, not the other way 'round!
She's the daughter of a duke! You don't mean...
No. I'm nobody.
Lady Cumberland and I are comrades, along with thousands upon thousands of Londiniers who long for peace.
I do hope you're one of them, Mr. Bader.

lines.st:
Act 4
——————
Scene 3

lines.x: All those loyal to this land stand with me. The final battle against evil lies before us.
Children... do you live here?
Hide not in the corners and shadows. Even if you cannot hold a sword...
There is more to being a warrior than simply holding a sword. Open your eyes. Watch, listen, think.
Tell me what is right, what is wrong, and what kind of life you wish to live.
I march onwards, with you as my witnesses.
Brilliant! Look how good Ralph is, Lady Golding... how much he's grown. And to think he was a mischievous little beastling just four years ago!
Yes, I'm delighted. A handkerchief, Molly?
Handkerchief? Oh, yes, I can't stain my clothes with tears in front of the children... Oh, but I just can't stop crying, to think that Ralph and the other kids will be leaving school so soon...
It's time for them to have a rest. And us, too.
Yes, the final scene... that's the best part.
Who's clapping?
You didn't invite anyone to the rehearsal today, did you?
The teachers cast their glances toward the back of the classroom.
A man in military attire claps slowly by the door.
What a splendid performance.
...Lt. Colonel Lettou.
I didn't think the Commander of the Defense Forces would grace this classroom with his presence.
Good afternoon, ladies.
What are you doing here?
I said as much, I'm here to enjoy the performance.
...Stay behind me, children.
I mean no offense, Lt. Colonel Lettou, but you're not welcome here.
No need to be so distant, Golding.
I've always approved of your efforts. The more difficult the times, the more important education becomes.
You're speaking as though you have nothing to do with these 'difficult times.'
You're always finely dressed, aren't you? No matter what the times.
The only difference is you have more and more medals on your collar now.
Helping the Military Commission govern Londinium is not an easy job. I've encountered my share of... difficulties.
But my love for this city has never changed. I'm just like you, in that regard.
I'm sure Tom the cobbler agrees.
Tom is a friend too. The memory of that incident pains me.
He got drunk and babbled something about 'His Majesty' and the 'Steam Knights.' He could hardy even spell 'Sarkaz.'
He's still alive.
Life is about more than simply being alive.
Are you accusing me of betraying Victoria, Golding?
I respect your courage, ladies.
But you're mistaken.
I came here to take another look at this school. Nothing more.
It's unfortunate that you seem to have forgotten me, Miss Molly.
Forgotten...?
I've spent just as much time here as you have. I even remember the children who came with you.
Unfortunately, I was no longer so free, after my graduation from the military academy.
Golding, you trusted me once. We discussed the history of Gaul, the eternal pillars of literature—
That will do, Lt. Colonel Lettou.
I'm grateful to your father for sponsoring the school over the years. He made efforts to light the torch of wisdom, to drive out ignorance and chaos.
I once thought you like him. But you've extinguished the tiny spark with your own two hands.
Is that so, Golding? Why do you think the school has survived this long?
Why, you—don't even dare threaten us!
I have no reason to threaten you. Certainly not with the children around.
We're all remnants of Gaul, Ms. Golding.
We know the merciless devastation of war, the snuffing of countless lives, and the destruction of human progress towards wisdom.
I don't want Londinium to be the next Lingones, nor do I want to see the wonderful play that I just witnessed disappear forever.
This is the burden that I have chosen to bear.
...I cannot endorse your views.
The Royal Opera House of Lingones has long since been reduced to ash, but 'Ode au Triomphe' continues to be performed around the world.
Buildings fall and metal giants rust, but what we created within never dies. It's our knowledge, our culture, our hope.
The darker the clouds of war, the more important it is that we hold onto our beliefs. Trust in beauty, and remind ourselves what separates us from the animals.
You believe you can win a conflict like this?
What you represent: fear, power, violence... these things can't keep everyone suppressed forever.
I must believe.
I can only believe.
As long as there is light, we'll follow it.
I envy you then, Ms. Golding.
Look up at the dark clouds above you. A storm is coming.
There is still time before the thunderbolt falls. We have a choice.
I hope to see the conclusion of the play... someday.
The Commander of the Defense Forces does not linger on the streets after leaving the school.
He came alone. There are none of his men in the vicinity.
She must find Adams and get the news out.
No matter how amicable the commander looked, there must be a reason why he came to the school.
Molly does not know, and neither do the children, but Golding does.
Why are there so many Sarkaz?
The path to the bookstore is a familiar one. She has tread it countless times in the past two years.
But today, the path seems much longer than she remembers. She cannot see the end of it. She remembers the day that the Sarkaz came.
Can she truly change?
I must believe. I must...
Fear seizes Golding by the throat.
She feels as though she is about to throw up. She turns and flees.
Lance! Patty!
The kids...! Nan—
Nngh—!
You need not have bothered yourself with a task as simple as this, sir.
Then you should have arrested Ms. Golding, Lettou.
It's what Manfred would have done. Old Red-Eye wouldn't have left a soul in the school alive.
We know you're not Sarkaz. You want to save one final shred of dignity for yourself, for the Defense Forces, for the people of the city.
But we believe this is safer.
Why you...
Lance, Patty... I hope the children are safe.
I'm scared, Lt. Colonel. Scared that people like you and the Sarkaz may hurt the children. Scared that the children could grow up to be like you.
You sound just like our unconscious guest here.
Yes... we're feeling.
She cares about those young Victorians more than she cares about herself. We can feel it, but we find it difficult to understand, especially since it does not arise from ties of blood.
...Sir?
We find you interesting. Through you, we understand ourselves... understand the Sarkaz better.
You want answers? About what?
You did not seem the kind of person to be curious about the Sarkaz, Lettou.
My apologies, sir.
The mission always comes first.
Take Miss Molly back, Lt. Colonel. Watch her closely. We will stay by Ms. Golding's side, and gather intelligence for the coming war.
Yes, sir.
What's the matter, sir?
Someone has discovered us.
Should I call the men?
No need. They can't stop him.
He has learned much from Theresa. We will have an... interesting conversation.
...Molly.
Hm?
Did anything happen while I was away?
No. Lance and Patty were scared. Ralph and the older kids took the little ones to get some rest.
Good. I've been thinking... the children will enjoy the play, won't they?
Our hard work will mean something?
What are you talking about?
No... nothing.
Don't mind the Commander of the Defense Forces, Ms. Golding.
I... I'm not feeling well. I need some rest.
Sorry, Molly. Could you clean up?
Sure.
There should be an end to the play. The Steam Knights arrived at the palace.
The children will perform the final scene in a few days, won't they?
...Absolutely.
There sure are lots of possibilities, so long as the conclusion of the story hasn't yet been told.
But even a play that's been performed can be changed. People always look for the latest.
You've been watching for a long time, haven't you?
The stage is right here. Why don't we start from the beginning, from act one?
Everyone dreams about how things would have been, if none of this had happened.
'What ifs' mean nothing.
But if there is even a remote possibility...
Why don't you join us on stage, sir?
Good morning, sir! There is a look of concern on your face. Is something worrying you?
The horn of victory has blown atop the walls for three days and nights, but why do I feel uneasy?
Has our great general not returned triumphant? Praise his bravery and fearlessness!
Bravery? Fearlessness? Perhaps.
A call to arms united our spirit, but the price was ruin and hate.
You should have served a king, but you gave your loyalty to a thief.
The mysterious spectator takes a step forward, his gaze moving from his counterpart to the distance.
He is not building up tension, but recalling memories of another battlefield, memories that have lain dormant for too long.
It is not a king that I serve, but honesty and courage.
Destruction awaits, if you stay on this path.
The destruction of the individual is nothing, compared to having to watch a tyrant bring this country to ruin.
How do you know her end, sir?
I can predict her end, as I know her beginning.
How could you reminiscence about the past, young as you are?
Who can keep the sun from setting? Such desires are the greatest greed.
I have seen countless acts of madness performed in the name of preserving this glory. They turn to banditry, or fight amongst themselves, ultimately accelerating the decay of this country.
'Molly' turns around onstage, takes a few steps, then turns back, hiding her face in the shadows.
But her counterpart speaks first. His expression is calm, like water undisturbed. He shifts his hands, then crosses them.
They will not admit that those who fall try to cling to the sunlight, but grasp only lightning.
Must they accept their fall?
They can choose to not look back at the peak.
If they would turn their gaze around, they would realize that it is no abyss lying before them, but fertile earth in which infinite possibilities lie.
You say the abyss can be conquered? You do not know the truth of the abyss, nor do you understand that time is short.
The abyss lies before us. We can fill it with flesh, and burn the remnants with blood, leaving behind wide open fields for those who come after.

lines.st: Only when the ashes of old are blown away can a new crop grow on the field, to feed the next generation.

lines.x: You saw it, Morgan?
The guards at the warehouse were so nervous that they didn't even see me coming.
Alright, alright, you win today. Take your jerky.
Ha, knew you'd be cross. Next time you can come with, and loser has to keep guard.
...They're staring at us.
Who?

What are you stari—
What's this about...? Get outta the way, already. I need a drink from the kitchen. Thirsty after all that work.
You shouldn't have done that.
You mean lure away the Sarkaz patrol, giving your people a chance to escape the warehouse?
Yes.
Funny way to say 'thank you'? Or did I hear you wrong?
Our comrades in Sudean are grateful, and so are we. The mission went well. But why did you have to burn the warehouse?
It belonged to a Victorian merchant, not the Sarkaz! The Sarkaz were just occupying it.
Ha. Those fat cats are as cowardly as they are greedy. They want to help the Sarkaz, I'm happy to make them suffer for it.
You do understand that you're just driving them into the arms of the Sarkaz?
Even better. More than just warehouses to burn for those traitors.
That's gangster talk, not the way of the Self-Salvation Corps.
Do you still insist on working with these people, Lady Isabelle?
My name is Dagda here.
Er...
It's just, Lady Isa—Dagda. When Lady Allerdale introduced you and Her Highness to us, we thought...
Siege told you on day one that it was the Glasgow Gang working with the Corps, not Her Highness Alexandrina and the Knight of the Tower.
Do you really want to see a hoodlum lead us, even take the throne?
What shit-for-brains gives you the right to say that about Vina?
Sword back in its place, Morgan! We're on their ground!
Many of us would rather follow you and Cumberland...
Stuff it! I-I mean, you've said your piece. As long as we still stand together, you should not speak ill of our fellows.
Sorry, I spoke out of turn.
You shouldn't have stopped me.
And let you make a mess for Vina to clean up? Not to mention the bunny and the Doctor from Rhodes Island. Next time, think about your company before you lose your head.
Ha... never thought the day would come that you'd be the one talking to me about losing my head.
Gotta change, and adapt to the environment. Look how Dagda is doing.
Don't say that, it feels weird.
You think I wanna congratulate you? Living your way doesn't feel too nice.
But there's one thing the past couple days've taught me, Morgan. We have to adapt to keep up with Vina.
...Hmph.
Here, your jerky. Neither of us won the day.
Keep it. I've got no appetite.
I've been thinking, Hannah... is this the kind of life that Vina wants? Since when did nobles and merchants give her the respect she deserves?
You're not thinking from the right angle.
They'll realize Vina is a good leader. And the respect Vina gets, she would've earned herself.
Ah, Lady Allerdale! Finally!
Sorry, I've been busy.
I heard you were looking for a buyer for the Steam Armor?
...The news reached you quickly.
Only rumors, only rumors. A friend mentioned that a suit of Steam Armor from Londinium might appear on the market.
But the transaction fell through at the last minute.
...I see.
I would not recommend that you sell something so valuable too easily. It's hard to get a good price under current market conditions.
Thank you for the advice. I'm just trying to do something useful with what little assets my family has left.
I understand. What little money we have means nothing to the Sarkaz.
Has the banquet not yet started? There's no need to wait for me.
We can't have that now. You've been the pillar of Central Londinium for ages. It's all thanks to you that we're alive today.
How is one even supposed to negotiate with a bunch of lunatics who don't care about money?
You flatter me, sir. I'm just a nobody who spoke up a few times at the unjust treatment that those like you have received.
Now, now, don't say that. House Cumberland will undoubtedly rise again under your leadership!
Yes, the past is the past now.
When Londinium knows peace again—not to say that she's not at peace right now, of course!
In any case, House Cumberland will have our gratitude.
Lt. Colonel Lettou is here!
We salute you!
It's an honor, Lt. Colonel. You have not graced us with your presence for many nights.
We are most grateful for your efforts to keep the peace!
I've come into a few bottles of excellent wine.
They may not have been so impressive in the old days, but in times like these they are a rarity. I'll have my butler bring them to your residence.
Ladies and gentlemen...
There will be no more banquets, from this day forth.
The Military Commission will be interviewing you soon. Good luck.
...Huh?
Wait, no! What do you mean?
Lt. Colonel Lettou! Lt. Colonel Lettou!
Lady Allerdale, you must speak to Lt. Colonel Lettou! This is serious! P-People will die!
Lt. Colonel! Please don't go!
Miss, this is...
The Sarkaz have been biding their time. They let these fools wallow in luxury in order to keep the dukes from coming together.
There is nothing any given duke would like more than to become the master of Victoria.
At the center of Victorian high society is Londinium, and whomever makes an enemy of Londinium nobility makes an enemy of the entire state.
Such an offense against Victoria would have forced the dukes to put aside their individual interests and join forces to march on Londinium.
But the Sarkaz don't care anymore.
They've achieved their goal.
Some think what befell Londinium the past few years a disaster. But now—
War has truly come.
What about us, then?
Could you talk to Lt. Colonel Lettou, as the nobles suggested? You might...
They called me 'pillar'. But whom amongst them set foot in Cumberland Manor before the Sarkaz took control of Londinium?
Which of the Duke's true friends participated in these pretentious banquets?
I'm simply a figurehead for nobles who lack chips on their side, or those who are trying to protect themselves.
They flocked to the Cumberland banner, so Cumberland may take the fall for them.
So what you mean is...
Let's go, Ailshie. Inform our Self-Salvation Corps and Rhodes Islander friends to evacuate the ducal manor immediately. It is no longer safe.
We'll take one last look at our ancestral home.
Allerdale.
You are...
A letter from the duke.
A few days in Auchterigg, then Highbury. Look at all those factories and houses packed tight together. Never seen a place so cramped.
Can we go back to Central Londinium after this mission, Captain?
And do what? I don't know what Manfred was thinking, hiring kids like you who've barely taken a life before...
I'm not a kid! I did a lot of jobs back in Columbia with Grimm, when he was still around!
Grimm, eh...? I have babysit now because he got himself killed in Sudean. I should've taken his head and nabbed the bounty beforehand.
Er... weren't you friends?
It's not my job to teach you how to be a mercenary, kid. But it is yours to read the map. Where do we go next?
Munitions Factory No. 11 is just ahead.
General Manfred gave us a pretty long list... these are all older workers who know something, right? Just take them somewhere with no witnesses and kill them?
I would kill everyone in the factory, if it were up to me.
All that crap about killing only the ones who know anything... does he have any idea how much trouble that is?
Well, he's the boss.
Whatever. Put away the list and let's go.
Feist!
Patto.
It's been a year. I missed you.
Sorry, I should have tried to send word.
Never mind that. I'm so glad to see you. For the past year, I've been telling everyone that you'd definitely come back alive.
Remember that vent over there? There's a bit of a platform outside the other end of it.
A few years ago, you said you would jump off it and onto the factory next door.
We were scared out of our minds. It was what, more than thirty meters off the ground? Fall, and you'd be dead for sure.
But you dared to jump. And then... Tommy jumped, Day jumped, and in the end I jumped too.
Whenever they ask me if I think you're dead, I tell them to look up at the vent.
Haha... we weren't afraid of anything, were we?
So these are your Self-Salvation Corps friends?
Looks well-read to me.
I heard there were all kinds in the Corps. Guess the rumors are true.
The Doctor is definitely well-read.
You have partners?
It's sounding more and more like the real deal, isn't it?
Haha.
So, why have you come back? Anything I can help with?
I—
Patto!

Ca—
Catherine... I'm sorry.
Get back to work.
Yes, ma'am.
But Feist is back, isn't he? He's not going anywhere?
Don't you worry about him right now. As for your friend, we should talk somewhere else.
Don't blame Catherine, Feist.
She's been protecting us from the Sarkaz for the past year. It's thanks to her that we haven't lost anybody.
There was one time when things got really bad, and she called on the leader of the Defense Forces to mediate.
But the Sarkaz have been putting more pressure on us, on Catherine.
I know, Patto.
I don't blame Granny one bit.
When I joined the Corps and truly understood what I was facing...
I knew what a difficult decision Granny had to make.
Alright, talk. What did you come back for?
We're looking for something.
Something?
Yes. A hidden Sarkaz supply line that's fueling their mad war.
We're certain the line ends here in the industrial district.
We need to find it.

opts: Feist says no one knows the district better.

lines.x: No one knows the district better than you, Granny. I'm sure you can help.
That's why I came to you.
Flattering me won't do you any good.
Yes, we won't cause any trouble for the factory.
Why do you think I would help you?
Maybe I already told Patto to call the Sarkaz mercenaries.
If that's the case... I'll have to flee with the others.
Tell me, how many died in that incident you caused in Sudean?
Do you remember their names? Their faces? Do you remember what they looked like when they died? Their last words?
Don't rush your answer, Feist. And don't try to bluff your way through it.
I don't know what this 'Doctor' of yours thinks, but I know you. You think you're smart, but being smart isn't enough to fool death.
Catherine! A bunch of Sarkaz just showed up at the door...
Doctor, we need to get out of here—
They say Lt. Colonel Lettou, a Defense Force officer, wants to see you. You're to be escorted.
...What?
George, watch these two and make sure they stay put.
Care for a smoke?
Don't bother trying to butter me up.
Lt. Colonel Lettou wants to see me?
Yes.
We've been meeting our targets. Nothing for him to worry about.
Whatever. He wants to see you. Come with us.
Give me a minute. I have something to take care of first.
We're not asking, old bag.
I have to put someone in charge. This is very sudden, after all.
We can wait a little bit... can't we?
(Whisper) I don't think the General wants a scene.
Ugh. You have ten minutes.
Good enough.
Granny, why are they looking for you?
None of your business.
But—
Shut up and come with me.
Feist thought he was getting a talking to.
He was ready, thinking about what he could say to move the heart of the stubborn old worker before him.
Catherine has seen too much. Her temper is harder than the machines in the factory.
His cleverness with words has never been of any use before Catherine. Her fierce gaze sees through all, turning his wits to mush.
Feist feels a familiar sense of helplessness.
He always turns back into a little boy before his grandmother. Clumsy, excitable, prone to saying something he instantly regrets. He can only wait for his grandmother to speak first.
But Catherine says nothing.
She leads Feist down a long path. When they finally stop, he glances at her expression, and surprisingly sees no hint of anger.
What's up ahead?
Nothing.
I remember this workshop. You used to come here by yourself. Why is the production line empty, though?
What did you think this place was?
The old workers wouldn't say, but Patto made a bet with Day that it's the secret workshop where they used to build steam armor.
Tell Patto he won the bet.
So this is really the steam armor... no, I don't understand...
I've followed you down this path a few times. I heard... I heard the machines running, and the conveyor belt moving.
They still run, if someone hits the switch.
Why was it abandoned? Because the Steam Knights disappeared? No, even without orders, these machines are too dear to be left idle.
Why... why indeed?
The aging worker pats the machine, as though she was asking the cold metal.
Feist realizes that all the machines are spotless, with nary a speck of dust on them. It is as though time has stopped in this secret workshop.
Do you know why I'm the only one who came here?
Cam, Mike, Branson... they should have been standing there. And Harvey, he'd be responsible for watching the thrusters.
When the production line moves as one... I guarantee you've never heard a song quite like it.
Harvey... isn't that Dad's name?
Yes.
Your dad was the smartest worker in the entire city. He came up with all sorts of crazy ideas.
I've only seen him in photos.
I wish you could have seen him in the flesh. You're like him... too much so, at times.
Harvey was an expert in steam engines. He was like an engine himself, going so fast that I couldn't keep up.
I should've kept up.
Didn't Dad go missing after getting caught in some business on the streets? That's what you said whenever I asked.
Some business... if what happened twenty-six years ago could be called as much.
Twenty-six years ago? Didn't His Majesty...
Yes. Londinium fell into chaos overnight, the nobles were at each other's throats, and the dukes led the way.
Lots of factories got hit, especially military ones making steam armor. We were forced to stop work.
Harvey... he was young then. Almost the same age as you now. He loved the factory, and he loved this city.
He left the factory with the other workers to protest against the nobles. He wanted the dukes to hear the voices of the workers, and protect our livelihood.
Naive, wasn't he?
I think back to it, on quiet nights... was it a cold one in December? Was his blood warm when it came out of his chest?
Dad did it... for his ideals.
You want to say how noble it is to sacrifice for your ideals?
The leaders of your Self-Salvation Corps and that 'Doctor' of yours... is that what they say when they mourn their dead friends?
It's what Commander Clovisia would say. As for the Doctor... the Doctor is even more quiet than usual at times like those. But I can feel the pure, heartfelt sincerity.
The dead are in no position to appreciate their greatness.
As for the ones they leave behind... these words of consolation are like cigarette smoke, light and fluffy, comforting for a moment, but when it leaves your lungs...
She takes a breath, a pause, then breathes out again.
The temperature is not low enough to leave a white fog.
Only one thing is for certain. Forty-one workers walked onto the largest street in Central Londinium on that night, twenty-six years ago. None came back.
Nothing changed about the city. The nobles, the wealthy, they pretend nothing happened, but this workshop would never be the same again.
I'm too old to train another group of workers as good as them, Feist.
They should've taken over for me. Harvey should've been the one to teach you all you knew.
Granny...
Believe it or not, I've never blamed Harvey, and I've never blamed you.
But think about it carefully. You should've thought about it carefully when you tried to lead Patto and the others into your rebellion.
If you survive this war, but the others don't, will you be like me, fraught with grief and regret night after night, leaning over machines with no one to use them?
I didn't understand your decision three years ago, Granny.
But now, when I think back to that day, when I try to put myself in your shoes...
It would have taken me a long time to make that decision.
You're right. Being smart isn't enough to fool death.
But compromise and subservience won't get the job done either.
Catherine takes a drag on her cigarette and says nothing.
I can't say I've thought this through.
I can't guarantee we'll be able to drive out the Sarkaz.
I've been feeling the weight on my shoulders, ever since Rhodes Island told us that we didn't have much time.
I'm afraid I'll do something stupid again.
But I can't stop here.
Otherwise, I'll never be able to face Johnny, Gabby, and the Self-Salvation Corps fighters who gave their lives.
Too young... too naive, little brat.
You there, 'Doctor'. You look like you've got a grip on yourself, unlike this boy.
Would you kindly take care of him?
There's a hidden compartment in the third drawer of my desk.
What?
The Sarkaz are watching closely. There's no place for you here. Read it and get out.
Don't bring trouble to anyone else.
...Thanks, Granny.
You catch a glimpse of Catherine's expression as she passes by you.
Sorrow, and gratification.
Let's take a look, Doctor.
Granny's room is over here.
Do you have the goods?
All here.
How's Mr. Bader?
I heard he's ill. Anyway, it's all here. Our business is concluded.
Good. Please pass on my gratitude.
One after another, heavily-armed mercenaries emerge from the warehouse.
Their leader walks towards the Self-Salvation Corps and extends the hand not holding a weapon.
Are you our new employer? I'm Totter.
Hello, Totter. Thank you for making the trip.
The container was a little stuffy.
Not that it's much different out here.
Hah. Welcome to Londinium.
Team 8, take our new friends to the base. Watch out for Sarkaz patrols.
Yes, ma'am. Come with us.
The scales tip ever so slightly in our favor.
We don't have much time left, Clovisia. They've began culling the nobles.
We've evacuated the ducal residence and moved to Hideout Beta, which is better concealed. It's safe for now, but I'm not sure for how long.
The number of Sarkaz in the city is increasing.
The bulk of their forces will return in three days.
The Nachzehrer King... The terrible corpse eater.
Amiya told me about his powers. He 'eats' war... turning the battlefield into part of himself simply by standing on it.
How do we defeat the battlefield itself?
The dukes' fleets must be united to win.
Our soldiers have to avoid him. That's why we need to move before he returns.
They're living legends.
The Sarkaz Royal Court... if they truly plan to suppress the entire city, over the heads of Lt. Colonel Lettou and the Defense Forces...
You're good at math, Clovisia. What are the chances we triumph over these terrible legends?
Very good then. The Self-Salvation Corps didn't come this far by crunching numbers.
The fighters are doing their best.
Whatever may come, at least our hearts are one, aren't they, Allerdale?
...Huh? Oh, yes, of course.
What are you thinking about?
I'm thinking... I hope tomorrow goes as well as these past few days have.
Don't you think they've been going a little too smoothly lately?
That's a good thing, innit?
Good things don't happen on their own.
I agree with Vina. You get lucky once, sure, but not every time.
Hey, those men have been loitering for a while...
The man on the corner glances at them briefly.
He should not have been able to see so deep into the alley. But he suddenly pulls back his gaze, and briskly walks away with his head lowered.
Dagda!
Yes?
Tell Allerdale... tell the Corps to evacuate the warehouse district immediately!
Yes, Siege.
They want to know why.
We are being watched... no time to explain.
Dagda, Indra, Morgan. Proceed with the plan.
Oh...
Is everyone out?
I... I don't know...
Run now. Talk later.
They're still on our tails.
You should go... someone of your station shouldn't risk her life for us...
Would you say the same if it was Allerdale or Clovisia?
...You're different.
Heads down.
Huh?
Siege's hammer swings through the air, barely missing the fighter's head.
A massive hole appears on the wall with the sound of bricks cracking. Within lies the severed hand of a Sarkaz caster, still holding his wand.
Different, am I?
You... you...
Stay close. I'm not in the habit of slowing down my fights.
We haven't shaken them yet.
These Sarkaz are persistent.
Their uniform is the same, but the Sarkaz of Sudean are like children compared to these.
They're different.
What should we do? Keep making circles in the streets?
You used to sit in front of a desk, didn't you?
Huh? Yes, I was an accountant... why?
How did you travel? By car?
Er, yes...
Do you know how wide a Londinium alley is? How long it takes to run from one end of Wellington to the other? How many sewer entrances there are on each block?
They do.
Who does?
Over here, Vina!
Target sighted!
Hostiles in the shadows!
This way, Vina!
I hear explosions coming from the safehouse.
The Corps fighters aren't moving quick enough. We have to cover their retreat.
Grr. This way!
Siege!
Dagda, what's the situation?
Clovisia evacuated with most of them. Allerdale...
...I'm fine.
You're hurt.
I can take care of myself.
You should not have returned, Your Highness.
They're inside.
Kill them all.
Forgive my bluntness, Siege, I don't think you should...
You don't think I should have come back either?
No, I don't think you should be at front, by yourself.
Allow me to stand by your side, from time to time.
Very well. Come forward, Dagda.
You need to rest, Allerdale.
The daughter of the Duke of Cumberland can't lose to the daughter of the Count of Manchester.
I know Allerdale and Dagda better.
Then I'll stand by your side, as your friend, Allerdale.
What about you, soldier? Can you stand?
Y-Yes...
Then grab your weapon and fight.
Londinium... this iron jungle belongs to us. It's time to show the Sarkaz who is hunter and who is hunted.
Your station won't save you, Aslan.
We don't care.
The Sarkaz future is now. You'll be buried with the past.
Wait...
Look out! Ambush, behind the shelves!
You're sharp.
Hey! Over here!
No, here!
Do it, Siege!
Now then.
Look out!
Flashy, but pointless.
Playtime's over. Drop your weapon and you can die a quick death.
Make things easier for both of us.
Sorry... I got careless.
No need to apologize.
The Sarkaz gazes at Siege, and Siege gazes at Allerdale.
She raises her hands slowly. Her hammer falls to the ground with a bang.
She winks at Allerdale, who raises the corner of her mouth.

Wait... wind? But we're indoors...
It's...
Damn it, it's Arts! Incoming!
Vina, catch!
Now then.
Gah—!
Are you alright?
I'm in a good mood, at least.
The warehouse is collapsing! Pull out! Surround them outside!
Your safehouse doesn't seem so solid.
Safehouses aren't meant to be impregnable fortresses.
Follow me.
The hidden passage should be blocked under the rubble, but we need to up our pace.
Ugh...
You need to get your wounds wrapped, Allerdale.
I... I'm fine.
Dagda, take the fighter back to the Self-Salvation Corps camp. We'll split up.
Your Hig—Siege.
You showed your leadership. Now it's our turn to show you what we can do.
Thank you.
Where are you going?
Allerdale's injuries can't wait. I'll find somewhere nearby...
Wait, I remember this place...
Surprised you still have an impression of this alley, Vina.
That afternoon, when you brought back the Sighs of Kings, with them.
...What?
Nothing. Let's keep going.
This path connects the palace to the ducal residence. Not many know of its existence.
I have assurances from one whose word carries weight that Cumberland Manor will be safe until tomorrow.
Enough for us to get these little flesh wounds treated.
Vina...
You knew I was there?
I know what sounds I should hear from outside my room.
My dear Lady Vina, staring at me won't heal the wounds on my back.
Would you kindly change the bandages?
...Right.
Ugh...
Sorry, was I too heavy-handed?
It's nothing. I just let my guard down when there aren't so many eyes on me.
You bled a lot... how many times did you get hit?
Most of them don't hurt anymore.
If I reacted faster... maybe it would have saved you a scar.
Escaping from those fighters was no mean feat.
Besides, I've been wanting to thank you for days.
You've helped the Corps a lot, and you even saved the old steam armor.
I have a responsibility, Allerdale. I... I don't want to see you lose anything else.
Hah.
No matter, Vina. If there's one thing I've learned over the years, it's that life usually doesn't go the way we want.
If it did, we wouldn't be hiding in this dark, little room, and we wouldn't be sitting with a bunch of stinking bandages.
We...
Let me think... we'd be sitting in a garden, taking tea together, discussing poetry and the weather.
We could go hunting. The alcornes are plentiful in the countryside this season. They run fast, but stop and stare when they see a crowd. Do you like hunting?
...I don't know.
I've almost forgotten how it feels myself. But I'm guessing you don't like dancing, at least not at those onerous dance parties in the social season. I don't like them either.
The dresses are so much stiffer than hunting attire, and so tight they rob you of your appetite for the banquet.
Ha, I can imagine.
And...
What?
I'm thinking the hunting outfit would look better on you.
Vina...
Yes?
Perhaps... we should have seen more of each other. Much more.
Fate robbed us of a beautiful life, against our will.
Luckily, we still have the future...
What's the matter? You look surprised.
...I'm talking about the future.
You don't do that often?
Back when I was running for my life, I hardly ever thought about the future. The past was in my dreams, and the future... it was in a fog, where I couldn't see anything.
This is not a flaw, Vina. It's a virtue.
Nobles like to talk about the future, but most of them are more concerned about the dinner menu.
It's not because they've grown numb to life, but because they understand how precious the present is.
Like the present now?
Yes...
Like the present now.
Ailshie.
Good morning, Lady Allerdale. Everything is in order.
The steam armor... why have you taken it out?
It's too big to take with us.
...Is that so?
Has Her Highness Alexandrina left?
Yes, she has her own mission.
Your injuries...
Are fine, thanks to Her Highness.
I haven't seen you smile like this in a long time.
Am I being too calm before the storm?
It does bring back long-forgotten feelings to stand with Her Highness and the others.
It's been some time since I encountered someone I could trust.
Her Highness Alexandrina saved this armor for you. I may be a lowly servant, but I feel a sense of gratitude.
It's the pride of Cumberland.
Pride...
The armor and your honored ancestors... I remember how you used to talk to them.
Children like to wallow in fantasy.
But I learned, twenty-six years ago, that it's no hero, just a broken hunk of metal.
We've all changed, yet the armor seems frozen in time.
Do you... like Her Highness Alexandrina?
Her Highness is very kind. I've seen how she treats her fellows, eating the same food and sleeping in the same room.
She's not like most nobles. She doesn't see them as her servants, nor will she abandon them.
Victoria is fortunate.
The heart of Her Highness is strong. Years of exile have not undermined her dignity and integrity.
You said Victoria. What about yourself, my lady? What do you think of Her Highness?
I hope she has a bright future.
So you...?
I got a letter last night. Two in the same day.
She's... impatient.
But you've reunited with Her Highness!
Do you remember how Her Highness came here with the Sighs of Kings, twenty-six years ago? It must've been a sign, and its portents might come true very soon!
We've waited for so long, Miss. You could...

lines.st: 'Allerdale Cumberland.
You will meet Vina again, someday.'

lines.x: He...
I've long since given up waiting, Ailshie.
How long has it been since Jim left?
He returned home to County Peninsula the year after the incident.
...Twenty-five years. We haven't had a gardener for a quarter century.
I used to love the bushes that grew there. They tripped me from time to time, but the tiny golden flowers that bloomed from them were so beautiful.
The first batch all died the winter after Jim left.
Mother sent people all around and learned that it was the noblest breed from Minos. The money it took to buy those seeds would have fed an ordinary family of Londiniers for half a decade.
Your garden remains beautiful, even without that particular kind of bush.
I tried, Ailshie. We all tried. You toiled tirelessly, summer and winter, in order to keep the garden in good shape.
But without those seeds, our garden would never return to its former glory.
I sent her a letter the fifth New Year's Day after Dad died.
Her reply included the seeds that House Cumberland could no longer afford.
Ailshie... we've always been living like this.
If the Duke was here...
...She always had tricks up her sleeve.
No, my lady, I meant your father.
Father... father.
I can hardly recall his voice now.
Perhaps it's only in this garden that I... that I remember how he held my hand, taught my how to swing a sword, knocked me down, then picked me back up and sat me on his shoulder...
I wanted to preserve them, many years ago, Ailshie. I tried everything I could. The garden, the armor... and the shadow of my father.
But there is nothing worth less than 'want'.
I remember how I wanted to become a Steam Knight when I was little.
Ailshie. Sometimes... just sometimes, I think...
Growing up is such a cruel thing, isn't it?
With the exception of a lucky few, we all become tired and unremarkable adults.
Always treading carefully, not daring to speak our minds, grasping at straws when the waves are rough, and never letting go of the ones we find.
What did you want to be when you were little, Ailshie? Not the servant of a fallen house, surely?
No, it's an honor to be by your side...
I want the truth.
Well... I suppose... I listened to a lot of bard's tales when I was little.
Ah. Ailshie the Bard.
Please don't tease me.
Growing up means toppling, crushing, turning to dust the possibilities that we thought were within our reach when we were little.
And here we are.
Can I preserve House Cumberland's glory? It passed beyond my reach a long time ago.
I, Allerdale, am who I am now.
...But even you cannot say who Allerdale will be, can you?
Will be...?
I'm old, Miss. I couldn't squeeze a tune out of an instrument, even if I had the chance.
But you promised me.
Maybe you thought it was just a childish fancy.
But I've always believed that you could do it.
To be a Steam Knight greater than Charles Lynch, greater than any of your ancestors.
You'll do it for me, won't you?
Enough, Ailshie! She wouldn't permit...
Then what do you want to do?
I'm behind you, no matter your choice.
I—
Take your time. You always make the right decision.
Miss... permit me walk around this garden, one last time.
It's most dear to me... even if it's just a shadow of its former self.
Allerdale wants to say something, but does not open her mouth.

She watches someone, with whom she has spent virtually every day of her entire life, walk towards the center of the garden. They have been through the worst of times together, and have been transformed them in ways they could not have imagined.
Allerdale realizes that she has not seen Ailshie smile like this for years.
She feels a sense of tranquility, one that she has not felt in ages.
If there is still a chance—
Suddenly, Allerdale's attention is drawn to a speck of orange in the distance.
It rapidly draws near, and she feels the temperature around her rise.
Ailshie! Quick, we must—
In an instant, the manor is engulfed in flames.
...Your Highness.
Quiet.
You're disturbing my enjoyment of the view.
Storied history turns to cinder. The proud manors of the nobles, reduced to charred ruins.
Tiles carefully sculpted and laid over centuries peel off one by one. Flowers collected from all the corners of the land wilt and wither.
My orders were to investigate the nobles.
Are you not happy with what I'm doing, 'Commander'?
Do you not enjoy the flames? Does the sound of blood evaporating not interest you?
'Hiss, hiss.' Listen and savor, Lettou, my friend.
I'm examining every drop of blood, as is my responsibility...
I will assist your investigation, ensuring that no... traitor... walks free.
As is my duty.

lines.st: There are some things that will never change, Ailshie!
I'll grow up to be a Steam Knight, just like Sir Lynch! No, even greater than Sir Lynch!
I'll protect you all, and I'll drive every scoundrel away.
I'll uphold the glory of Cumberland, just like my great-great-great-great grandmother!
I promise this, Ailshie! You'll see!

lines.x: Ailshie!
Hold on! I, I'll...
I promised that I'd...
Go, Allerdale.
The Vampire is coming. He cannot find you.
Ailshie is here. I have to—
You have to follow her orders. She will not allow you to deviate.
Y-You can't...
We can. The Duke has ordered us to help you 'complete your mission', but we are not your servants.
Do not forget her instructions.
Do not forget your place.
There is only one thing that you must do. Fulfill your duty.
For an instant, Allerdale forgets where she is, and who is with her.
She wants to run, towards the fire that is burning away at what little remains of her life.
Just like that night twenty-six years ago, when she wanted to run towards another fire to stop Parliament's men from taking her father.
That is your sole responsibility.
Allerdale's eyes open wide. The tears in them evaporate before the fire.
But she sees a great silhouette is rising in the burning garden.
Is it the damaged suit of steam armor? The steam armor upon which she placed so many dreams and hopes?
Return to the person of interest. Now.
Through so many trials and tribulations, Allerdale had prayed that her great ancestor would descend into the steam armor and help her overcome all challenges.
Now she sees the ancient armor breathing the flames of hell.
She sees the shadow in the fire roar towards her.
It is not her hope. She has strayed far from it.
Even if her ancestor truly returned—
Allerdale knows it could only be her worst nightmare.
Tell her.
I will obey her will, as always.
I, I don't understand, Your Highness...
I thought you were pleased with our hospitality...
You even graced us with your presence at our banquets, did you not? You liked this place!
I will not deny that you have brought me joy, from time to time.
I find your petty flattery and exaggerated cordiality adorable.
You must be mistaken! I have no links to the dukes or the resistance! I swear...
Any trouble must be the work of the Cumberland woman! I'll expose her!
Sanguinarch... Your Highness... please, I'll give you anything! My collection, my money... take all of it! Just...
What good are those to me, my dear?
You wouldn't kill me, would you? We should hold a wine tasting! You haven't sampled my private reserve yet...
I'll be alright, won't I, Lt. Colonel Lettou? This is Londinium!
They—the Sarkaz are just here to protect Londinium at the invitation of the Duke of Cavendish? They wouldn't kill an innocent, of course?
Take him to the front.
I tire of the noise. Begin.
B-Begin what?
Run in when you hear the order.
Confirm everyone is dead and you can come back alive.
In... into the fire? A fire that big? N-No! I'll burn to death!
You're going to do exactly that.
The ancient Sarkaz steps on the Heart of Victoria and raises a finger.
Argh—!
He did not see what was chasing him.
As the Sarkaz warrior said, he follows the order instinctively, running forward like a man possessed.
In the fire, the slightest chance of survival. On his tail, the scent of certain death.
But he only takes a few steps before stumbling in confusion.
Blood spurts from his body.
It no longer belongs to him. It serves another master now.
At its new master's command, it charges forth towards the collapsing manor, towards the raging flames, chewing through the ruins, swallowing the rubble, covering the cinders.
Nothing can stop the march of blood, not even fire.
He was right, Lettou.
I haven't sampled his private reserve.
We should, shouldn't we?
This is it. Wait here.
Lund, Charlie...
This is where you kill us.
...How do you know?
Simple.
You never told the workers what you were building for the past two years.
But a factory boss gets a few clues.
I guessed you were close to the final stages, the way you people have been pressing us lately. And that means it's time for those who know too much to die.
You lied to me.
Just getting my affairs in order.
What could lowly workers like us do, after groveling for the past three years?
Don't you dare try anything.
You, you and you. Come with me; we'll take a look around.
Keep a close eye on her, Paprika.
Alright.
Can I have a smoke?
Hm... okay.
Thanks.
Did you ever consider running?
Where?
And anyway, it'd only make trouble for the workers left behind.
I've been thinking about what you'd do to us. Looks like whoever's giving the orders still has something of a heart.
But... what do you mean?
They said to get rid of us quietly so the workers don't panic, didn't they?
If my life buys a chance for the others to live, then so be it.
You think the rest of the old timers you brought here don't get that?
I don't get it...
Huff...
How old are you, girl?
Doctor, do you know the story of the Steam Knights?
Granny used to build them.
It was a point of pride for us.
With our own hands, we built the armor worn by the defenders of Londinium, the ones who defended our home.
I wanted to see the birth of those great things, just like Granny... to take part in it.
Granny's been through a lot. She saw how Londinium became what it is today. And I can see how disappointed she is.
But I don't want to give up so soon.
Must be this drawer.
Hidden compartment... hidden compartment... ah, here it is.
Granny's incredible. I'd never know this desk had been altered, if she hadn't told me.
This is... my badge?
And a notebook?

lines.st: I don't know who I'm writing this for, what I'm writing this for.
Harvey died before Feist was born.
Lindy tried to understand, but her sorrow turned into anger towards me. She blamed me for not stopping Harvey that day.
I couldn't argue with her. I couldn't blame her for leaving Feist behind.
I heard she remarried someone decent. Good for her.
I never told Feist. I didn't want him to know that his father died for nothing, and that his mother left with hate in her heart.

lines.x: So you grew up in Columbia.
Yes, my grandma raised me.
Mom and Dad were both mercenaries, but they're dead.
Why did you become a mercenary, then?
To feed myself, and grandma.
Sarkaz aren't welcome most places, so I had to be a merc.
What a good girl.
So you agree with the Regent?
You mean with occupying the city?
Hmm... I don't know.
We do what we're paid to do. I didn't know we were going to occupy such a big city.
In Columbia, we were helping merchants smuggle goods that they didn't want people to know about, or hunting wild beasts.
But I can tell you, that was the first time I saw Grimm so excited. He said the Sarkaz would get to live a decent life, if we were successful.
Grimm? The smoker?
Yes.
Where's he now? I haven't seen him around in a while.
He's dead.
Aren't you sad about that?
He always said that death and parting are a normal part of the mercenary life.
Death and parting. All these years of chaos, and the Victorians haven't learned the slightest thing about the Sarkaz.
Catch, girl.
Huh?
Catherine takes a little thing from her cigarette box and tosses it at Paprika.
It is small, soft, made of wool, and bright in color.
This is... a finger sleeve...
Grimm...
You made it for the old merc, didn't you?
His finger hurt because of an old wound, but he couldn't cut the smoking habit. I saw him hide it in his cigarette box.
Guess he didn't want to get it dirty, even though you made it for him to use.
He gave me the box when he left. Payment for smokes, those few months.
He never talked about you, but the moment I saw you, I knew you were the girl he was always thinking about.
I... I don't...
I don't know what he thought about the war.
But I know, without hearing it from him... you don't know what war is.

lines.st: I had a feeling Feist would leave.
He's too much like Harvey.
He thinks he can solve every problem with his wits.
Seeing him reminds me of Harvey.
I can't tell if I'm disappointed because the protest failed, or because Harvey didn't understand what the nobles were made of.
Maybe a little bit of both.
I let Feist go.
I knew I couldn't stop him.

lines.x: Doctor, I thought about what I was going to say to Granny, before I brought you back.
I thought I was prepared. We've seen so much in our time.
But I think I owe her an apology.

opts: She doesn't need your apology, does she?

lines.x: Catherine always looks strict and solemn. People forget that she's just an ordinary woman, who lost her livelihood.

lines.st: The Sarkaz don't let workers into certain areas of the factories. They're definitely preparing some sort of weapon.
They shouldn't have the resources to hold out this long, not with the dukes breathing down their necks.
But from the material being sent to the factories, with what I know of their situation, all the signs point to them having their own supply line.
None of this was supposed to concern me.
All I can do is protect the workers.
But—
I noted things I shouldn't have.
Maybe, deep down, I thought I could do more than I did when I watched Harvey go out into the streets, if Feist came back to me for help someday.
Workers aren't allowed near it, but I know the place too well.
There are only a few places suitable for a supply depot.

lines.x: Here, Doctor! Granny circled some places on the map.

opts: I hope we get results on the other side.

lines.x: Yes, it'll be hard to get close to the depots.
This information will help, though, even if we can't get close.
I wish I could say goodbye to Granny.
Guess it'll have to wait until next time.
Let's go, Doctor.
A lot of us don't actually know what we're fighting for.
It's just, we don't have a place to go. We're not welcome anywhere.
The King of Sarkaz promised us a home, a country, a future.
I've spent time with you people. I can tell that some of you haven't actually fought before.
What about you?
I can fight!
Have you killed anyone, girl? And I'm not talking about a villain you can kill without remorse... I mean real people.
You're just a worker. You haven't killed anyone. Don't talk to me like you know what it means.
You've been in Londinium for a while. You've heard of steam armor, haven't you?
I heard it's a powerful weapon that hasn't been seen for a long time.
Yes. It's a killing machine.
I built killing machine after killing machine with these hands right here.
I don't feel ashamed, because those machines were used to defend the land I lived on.
What about you, girl? Do you know what your weapon is for?
George, everyone willing to go is here.
What about Tommy?
No sign of him.
That's fine. Volunteers only.
George.
You found what Catherine left for you, Feist? Was it what you were looking for?
Yes.
I'm glad.
Now get going, before you can't.
Wait, what's going on?
Do you know why the Sarkaz took Catherine?
She knew too much. They're executing workers like her.
Something didn't feel right, so I had Day climb to the top of the factory to check.
What...?
Catherine kept it from us. She thought we didn't know anything.
But we aren't stupid. We guessed what's going on.
What are you gonna do?
Nothing in particular.
We've put up with the Sarkaz for a long time.
More or less gotten used to it.
Some folk think the Sarkaz will let us go, so long as we put up with them a little longer.
Now they want to do away with Catherine.
You'll die.
Better than standing around and watching them kill Catherine.
No one standing here has anything left to lose.
Stop.
You're with the Self-Salvation Corps.
We've heard about you. Lots of us are happy for you.
You've got more important things to do.
Don't let Catherine's sacrifice be in vain.
Doctor, I may have gotten some information from Granny...
But as the leader of this factory, Granny knows more about the Sarkaz production lines than anyone else.
She has significant strategic value, doesn't she?
...I request help from Rhodes Island.

opts: I know you're watching... here with me.

lines.x: Here.

opts: I'm sure you've identified the hostiles in this sector.

lines.x: They've tightened defenses, leaving gaps on the outside.
It's mostly loosely-organized mercenary bands guarding the outer area.

opts: Distract the mercenaries and take out the sentries.

lines.x: On it.
Thank you, Doctor, Ascalon.
Feist takes a deep breath.
He sticks his badge to his chest and goes in amongst the workers.
Don't make any rash decisions, Feist.
I'm not here as a member of the Self-Salvation Corps.
I'm here as the grandson of Catherine, as someone who grew up in this factory.
I belong here.
Just like you, I don't want Catherine to die.
I'm not gonna let her die, and I won't let you die either.
The Doctor's people will cut off Sarkaz communications.
Once we rescue Granny and the other factory bosses, we'll have plenty of time to get out of Highbury.
I'll lead you to the Corps.
Everyone will be safe.
...Everyone should be safe.
The workers exchange stunned looks.
But they see the fire of hope beginning to burn in each others' eyes.
One by one, they get behind Feist.
They have decided to trust Feist.
Feist! I-I'm going too!
Tommy... thanks.
But don't.
Why?
You hesitated because your dad's not well, right?
But—
And there's something I need you to do.
No matter the outcome, you will report to the Sarkaz that we staged a riot.
But...
It's important. I have to keep the ones who stay behind safe.
...Alright.
Let's go, then.
We'll get the old hands back.
Right!
Catherine smokes her cigarette quietly.
She watches as the Sarkaz drag one familiar face after another to the makeshift firing range.
She sees on those familiar faces anger, disgust, resignation.
Then, in the end, peace.
She knows her turn will come soon.
She feels a sense of calm that she has never felt before.
Feist was right.
Compromise and subservience will solve no problems.
She must pay for her choices.
But she got to see her grandson one last time, and left him a little gift, before she had to pay.
That's enough.
You're up, grandma.
Take her.
I can walk on my own.
Oh. Paprika.
Grimm never had you kill anybody, did he?
...Never.
Here's your chance.
Learn to kill.
This is how the Sarkaz treat a kid?
I heard what you said to her. You're right. She doesn't know what war is.
I didn't want to know what war was, if I could help it. No Sarkaz does.
But you left us no choice. None of you did. That's why it's time for her to learn.
I like that look in your eyes, grandma.
It's the same look I've seen on buddies who died with their eyes wide open.
I've killed a lot.
But it doesn't sit right with me, killing someone who can't fight back.
You can go back where you came from, if you don't have the stomach for it.
How do you know these workers didn't build the weapons that were used to kill your own people?
Morals are for peacetime. This is war. It's an argument we never got to have on fair, equal ground.
I don't need you to tell me that. I just...
Huh? A drone?
Now!
This is the place! I've scanned the area with the drone!
Move!
C-Can't I let someone else do it?
No.
I'm sure Grimm taught you not to question your captain's orders.
Either you kill her, or drop your weapon and I kill you.
Do it.
But...
Do it!
I... I can't!
...I always said Grimm was too soft on you.
We're not here to convince the Victorians to give our homeland back. If you can't do it, you're going to get it—
Huh—a drone?
Captain! The workers are revolting!
What?
Feist, we've got the Doctor giving orders here. We'll keep the mercenaries pinned down. Go and get Catherine!
Alright!
Are you out of your minds?
No, we're not.
Do you know how valuable an experienced worker is?
They're in high demand, you know.
Feist! You—
Hmph...
You could have lived longer if you pretended not to know anything.
Too bad Granny always says I never know when to shut up.
You think you can beat me?
No, but maybe she can.
Wha—
Umph...!
Bollocks, that thing was heavy...
Thanks, George. That felt good.
Thank Feist.
What about you, Sarkaz? Not gonna fight?
...Girl.
Your captain was right. This isn't the time to talk about right or wrong. We can't.
But if you don't know what you're fighting for...
Y-You can't go...
Guess I've got no choice then.
Charge!
What's going on? Where is everybody?
I'm gonna have to report this, if I don't get a response—
Argh...
The Sarkaz warrior feels weightless.
In his blurring vision, he sees a black figure just before he collapses.
It was another Sarkaz.
Why... Sarkaz...
Last one.
Ascalon is talking to herself, but she puts away the weapon with complete confidence that she is right.
She can clearly see the angry workers routing the Sarkaz mercenaries from her position.
Violence without strength of numbers was easily broken before years of pent-up anger.
The balance of power is reversed. Memories float to the surface.
She casts her gaze in another direction.
The center of Londinium.
We've tied up all the mercs.
How many injuries?
Not many. The mercenaries with this kid were in a panic and slow to react.
They didn't even make up their mind to fight.
W-We—!
We... we just...
You tried to resist, but you were ambushed by cunning Londiniers.
Their way, only a few old timers had to die.
Now the Sarkaz will have all your heads.
Is this your clever idea, Feist? Put all these years of patience to waste?
No.
If we watched you sacrifice yourselves to save our skins today, there'd be another group of victims tomorrow.
Until everyone only cares about themselves.
And... that's not your choice to make for all of us.
You're right. But it's not yours either.
This time, though, everyone came to rescue you of their own accord.
The Sarkaz have occupied the city for years... life is hard, but there are some red lines we can't let them cross. If we didn't come here today, we—
The Sarkaz will eventually boil us all.
George... you too.
It's not all of you here. That means there are some who don't want to see me come back alive.
Reality is crueler than you think, Feist. Right and wrong isn't so simple.
But what's done is done...
Catherine looks at Feist. It seems like she wants to say something.
She does not, however.
Let's go.
Guard this place.
Watch your formation, keep an eye on her movements.
You know who it is we're facing. Don't be like those stupid mercs.
...Here she comes.
Watch her hands.
She could draw her sword at any—
Watch the lights and shadows. Don't try to follow the sword.
The Sarkaz with white horns silently carries her sword in her arms.
Wordlessly, she keeps moving forward.
The guards instinctively step back.
Hold your ground!
She stops.
Under the gaze of the guards, she raises her left hand, and takes out a small piece of paper from within her tattered robe.
I came as appointed, as he wished. No need to be on guard.
Which way to Riverbank Street?
Shining!
What are you doing standing there? Get out of the way! I told you we had a family meeting today.
Understood.
What took you so long, Shining? I've been waiting!
...Salus.
It's not time yet.
No, it's not. The leader won't be angry.
Come in, dinner's ready. Look at you. Have you not had anything decent to eat since you left Londinium?
I've talked to the leader. Tonight's menu is being prepared to Royal Victorian Academy of Sciences banquet standards.
Oh, Shining, Shining! How long has it been since the old days, when we used to chat at the dinner table? Tell me what you've seen and heard!
Leader, Shining's back!
Welcome.
Sit. This is your place. We just came to Londinium then... it was a long time ago.
It has been empty ever since you killed those poor souls and fled with the test subject.
That betrayal chilled the clan to the bone.
But I don't blame you. Sit.
I can't wait. I've hardly had a good meal lately, staying all night in the labs.
She pulls a chair and sits by the right hand side of the white-horned Confessarius leader.
He stops speaking and sits with his eyes half-closed.
A moment later, Shining puts down her sword and walks toward the only chair that is empty by the long table.
Talk about your experiments, Salus.
Work at the dinner table? Alright, alright.
There isn't much to talk about. I'm doing my best to extract emotions from memories, but the voices of the dead don't linger long on my fingertips.
I've gotten some results, but only fragments. I'm running into a bottleneck.
The information is like branches cut from their tree. You can reconstruct the shape of the tree, but a tree can't survive without its roots, much less grow new sprouts.
And I can't capture voices from further before, never mind bringing them back to the present.
Like time, life only goes in one direction. A description of its nature must necessarily be abstract and literary. Even the Originium Arts of you and Shining can only capture an instant.
The King of Sarkaz is the exception.
That knowledge and the technique are like a waterfall, yet we can never find its source, or draw fresh water from it.
My research has stalled since Shining's experiments stopped.
What about you, Leader? Is the Regent planning something big? You've been spending most of your time at Westhaleg or The Shard; you hardly come to my lab anymore.
The Regent's plan is at a crucial stage.
I'm not sure I understand.
From Kazdel to Kazdel, the Confessarii's service to those in power has always been one of pure mutual gain.
But you've been so close to the Regent that people on the outside are beginning to think the Confessarius is the Regent's lieutenant.
Since when have we cared, Salus?
This is hardly the first time they've misunderstood the Confessarii.
What's different about Theresis?
I would not recommend saying that name within earshot of General Manfred.
That's why I'm doing it in front of family.
Like his predecessor, he came from no royal court, and his bloodline is far less pure than yours or Shining's.
None of their ancestors have ever caught the Confessarii's attention. We've never gathered memories from their bloodlines.
Even if the King of Sarkaz chose his sister, it does not mean that her brother...
Just as I've told you, Salus.
Power comes from blood. Blood inherits memory. Memory accumulates sin. Sin forms shackles. Only those unbound by history can truly unshackle power.
Alright, alright. May the glory of old return to the land under their majestic guidance.
No, Salus.
Succeed or fail, the Regent will make an impact on the Sarkaz. This is what is most frequently ignored.
By the way, the test subject you took... what's her name again? Lisa? Liz? How's she doing?
...Liz is doing fine.
Is Oripathy taking its toll on her?
She is... unusual. Even if Dame Kal'tsit is at... at... at that medical institution you found, they may not be able to truly help her.
It's good that she's back in Londinium. Your lab's still here with everything. You can bring her back anytime.
Is the food not to your taste, my dear sister?
It's been such a long time since you've been home. Speak up.
If this is about Liz... I apologize. But you know Liz is not your average Infected.
Who's responsible for her suffering and consciousness? Who's responsible for her pain and memories? Can you continue to get along after she remembers all that?
She's not family. You have to—
—She's my family.
But... but she's going to become an empty shell if you don't come back to us. Don't forget why she was built that way.
If you really care for her, I hope the end won't be too painful. Come back.
I'm sure you understand.
You understand. That's why you answered my invitation, isn't it?
From Originium to the truth of the King of Sarkaz. We've reached a clearer conclusion.
She's simply an old cage. We could build a new one, if we had to...
Enough.
Sigh.
I've never seen you really angry, even when you hurt your own colleagues and took the girl.
I guess I don't need to ask what you went through, all these years. What I can see is that...
You see outsiders as more important than family.
Salus empties her cup, which contained only water.
There was no wine. She does not like drinking, especially at work.
